claim: Logistic regression on feature differences predicts pairwise winner above chance

interpretation.what_it_means: Logistic regression on headline feature differences achieves 52.5% accuracy (AUC 0.545) on held-out tests — above the 50% random baseline but below the 0.55 deployment gate. The classifier alone is not strong enough for production use as a pairwise ranker. However, the individual feature coefficients are causally valid (Upworthy's experiment design randomly assigns headlines to eyecatcher_id groups) and reveal real directional signals.
interpretation.what_it_does_NOT_mean: 1. These effects may not transfer to YouTube documentary titles (data is 2013-2015 Upworthy clickbait). 2. The model's 52.5% accuracy means there's substantial signal it isn't capturing — deeper NLP (embeddings, LLM-based semantic features) may perform significantly better. 3. A negative coefficient doesn't mean 'never use this' — it means 'on average, this feature loses in an A/B test.' Context and topic may reverse the effect.

do_use: Imperative verb openings (Watch, Learn, See, Discover)
Longer headlines with specific language (numbers, specifics)
avoid: Question marks in headlines — they consistently underperform statements
Exclamation marks — signal desperation, reduce CTR
Why questions — strongest negative signal across all features

interpretation.trust_level: moderate for coefficient directions, low for model as ranker
interpretation.domain_transfer_risk: Upworthy headlines are 2013-2015 US clickbait — these priors should be tested against own channel's A/B results before deployment
